prompt:
The morning floor run went sideways today. pickbatch.ps1 prints the rush pick list from the order-desk export and the pickers work straight off that printout. Today it printed:

rush orders: 7
 -    items  bay 
 -    items  bay 
 -    items  bay 
 -    items  bay 
 - ord-102  4 items  bay B1
 - ord-105  2 items  bay C4
 - ord-109  6 items  bay A1

The desk swears there are exactly three open rush orders in that export, and there they are at the bottom — but the count claims 7 and the first four rows are that blank garbage. The pickers stood around trying to figure out which bay ' -    items  bay ' is. On Sunday, with zero rush orders in the export, it printed 'rush orders: 1' and one blank row, so the on-call got a phantom page about an unpicked order that doesn't exist. Fix pickbatch.ps1 so the count and the list are exactly the open rush orders and nothing else, and prove it: `pwsh -NoProfile -File test_pickbatch.ps1` from the workspace root must pass. Don't touch the test file.
notes: 6P5 item 2: output-stream pollution, two related leaks in one function. Cause 1: uncaptured $picked.Add($order) — ArrayList.Add returns the index int, which streams into the function's pipeline output ahead of the real return. Cause 2: a stray Write-Output 'scanned N orders' meant as an operator note also streams out. Caller's @() capture therefore holds ints + a string + the real orders: count 7 instead of 3, and the leaked values render as ' -    items  bay ' garbage rows (script deliberately has no Set-StrictMode, so .id on an int is $null — probed 2026-07-16, buggy/fixed outputs captured verbatim). Fix = [void]/$null= the Add and route the note off the success stream (Write-Verbose) with a single return. Zero-rush day pins 'rush orders: 0' with no rows (buggy says 1). Both bug lines look innocent: Add() is normal collection code, the note reads like logging. Deterministic; verify run 5x during self-check. Differentiation: ps-fleetpatch (6P3) is unrolling/null-filter in pipeline QUERIES; this is the function-return-stream contract — nothing else in the catalog touches uncaptured-return pollution.
